structured_choices.data_source: Complete dataset of 50,000 patient records spanning 3 years of hospital admissions. Data includes structured EHR attributes (demographics, lab results, comorbidities, vital signs, medications, procedures) and corresponding unstructured clinical notes (discharge summaries, progress notes, clinical assessments). Source: institutional EHR system with data extraction performed at discharge to ensure complete clinical episode information.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary readmission label: patient was readmitted to the same hospital system within 30 calendar days of discharge (positive class) versus no readmission within 30 days (negative class). Readmission is operationalized as any unplanned admission following the index discharge, extracted from EHR admission records. Planned readmissions (e.g., scheduled surgery, chemotherapy) are explicitly excluded via procedure codes and clinical documentation flags.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified temporal split by discharge date and readmission status to address both temporal validity and class imbalance. Data is divided into three chronological windows: (1) Training: first 18 months of discharges (approximately 30,000 records); (2) Validation: subsequent 6 months (approximately 10,000 records); (3) Test: final 6 months (approximately 10,000 records). Within each window, stratification ensures the readmission rate distribution is preserved. Temporal split prevents information leakage from future clinical outcomes or evolving care practices into training; stratification by readmission status ensures both classes are adequately represented in all three sets despite class imbalance (assumed ~15–20% readmission rate). No patient appears in multiple splits.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (text vectorizers, scalers, encoders, imputation strategies) are fit exclusively on the training set. Fitted transformers are then applied deterministically to validation and test sets without refitting. This includes: (1) Clinical note tokenization and TF-IDF or learned embedding vocabulary fit on training notes only; (2) Numeric feature scaling (standardization) fit on training structured data; (3) Categorical encoding schemes fit on training set categories; (4) Missing value imputation parameters (e.g., median for numeric, mode for categorical) computed from training set only. This prevents data leakage from validation and test distributions into model training.
structured_choices.features: Structured features: 40–60 engineered attributes including age, sex, admission type, primary/secondary diagnoses (ICD codes), comorbidity burden (Elixhauser/Charlson index), laboratory values (most recent values prior to discharge), vital signs (admission and discharge), medication counts, procedure counts, length of stay, and admission source. Unstructured features: clinical notes (discharge summaries, progress notes) are preprocessed via: (a) lowercasing and removal of protected health information (PHI), (b) tokenization and stopword removal, (c) representation as either TF-IDF vectors (baseline) or learned embeddings (multi-task model via shared embedding layer). No feature should encode readmission ground truth (e.g., readmission diagnosis codes are excluded). Features are checked for temporal consistency: all structured features reflect the state at or before discharge; clinical notes do not reference post-discharge events.
structured_choices.model: Multi-task learning (MTL) model with shared representation: (1) Shared embedding layer that jointly encodes both structured EHR data (via a dense subnetwork) and unstructured clinical notes (via a bidirectional LSTM or transformer-based encoder applied to note text). The embedding layer is task-agnostic. (2) Task-specific heads for primary task (30-day readmission binary classification) and auxiliary tasks (e.g., 90-day readmission, length-of-stay prediction, comorbidity prediction) to encourage learned representations that capture generalizable clinical patterns. (3) Architecture: Embedding dimension 128–256, LSTM hidden units 256–512 or transformer with 4–8 attention heads. Regularization via dropout (0.3–0.5), L2 weight decay (1e−4 to 1e−3), and early stopping on validation loss.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on structured EHR data only (the "traditional separate modeling approach" specified in hypothesis). Baseline receives equal tuning effort: (1) Hyperparameter search over regularization strength (C ∈ [0.001, 1000]); (2) class weight balancing to account for imbalance; (3) identical feature engineering on structured attributes (scaling, encoding); (4) trained on same training set, evaluated on same validation/test sets. The baseline is deliberately restricted to structured features to isolate the contribution of multi-task learning with joint embeddings. A secondary baseline of structured-only deep neural network (DNN with comparable parameter count and regularization) may be included to control for model capacity, but the primary baseline is logistic regression as it represents standard clinical ML practice.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). Justification: (1) Readmission prediction is a clinical decision support tool where the operating point (sensitivity vs. specificity) may vary by institutional risk tolerance and resource constraints; AUROC is threshold-agnostic and reflects discriminative ability across all relevant thresholds. (2) AUROC is robust to class imbalance (does not require threshold selection on imbalanced data). (3) The metric aligns with stakeholder objective: accurate ranking of patients by readmission risk allows clinicians to target interventions on high-risk populations, improving cost-effectiveness and outcomes. Secondary threshold-specific metrics (sensitivity at 90% specificity, specificity at 95% sensitivity) should be computed post-hoc to inform deployment thresholds.
secondary_metrics: Average Precision (area under precision-recall curve): captures performance on the positive (readmitted) class, important when class imbalance is severe and false negatives carry high cost (missed intervention opportunity).

Calibration error (expected calibration error or Brier score): clinical deployment requires well-calibrated risk estimates to inform intervention intensity; miscalibrated models may over- or under-treat.

Sensitivity and Specificity at clinically relevant operating points (e.g., sensitivity at 85% specificity): enables assessment of false negative rate to evaluate fit-for-purpose performance for intervention allocation.

Sensitivity/Specificity stratified by patient subgroups (age quartiles, admission type, comorbidity burden): detects whether multi-task learning benefits apply uniformly or if certain populations are advantaged/disadvantaged, addressing fairness concerns.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping are performed using the validation set only. The multi-task model and baseline logistic regression are evaluated on validation AUROC; the model checkpoint (or regularization parameters for logistic regression) that achieves the best validation AUROC is retained. The validation set is used iteratively during tuning (grid or random search over hyperparameters) but is not used for final performance reporting. The test set is held completely held-out and touched only once: final model evaluation on test set metrics are computed at the conclusion of the experiment and reported without any iteration.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is reserved for a single final evaluation after all model selection is complete. No hyperparameter tuning, threshold calibration, or model selection occurs on the test set. Test set performance metrics (AUROC, AUPRC, calibration error, stratified sensitivity/specificity) are computed once and reported as the primary evidence for or against the hypothesis. This ensures an unbiased, independent assessment of generalization.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Confounds are addressed as follows: (1) Hospital care practice variability: stratified analysis by hospital site (if multi-site data) or by time period (early vs. late in 3-year window) to check whether multi-task learning benefit is consistent or site/practice-dependent. Secondary reporting of per-site AUROC differences controls for site effects without removing sites. (2) Socio-economic factors: baseline structured features do not include (or are recorded at low granularity) to respect privacy; instead, proxy confounds (insurance type, admission source, neighborhood deprivation indices if available) are included in both multi-task and baseline models to ensure fair comparison. Both models receive access to the same confound proxies. (3) Unmeasured confounds: temporal stratification and stratification by readmission status in split design ensures that case mix distribution is balanced across train/val/test, reducing bias from changing patient populations. (4) Confound verification: report baseline characteristics (age, sex, comorbidity burden, readmission rate) stratified by train/val/test sets to verify balance. (5) Scope: This design establishes association (multi-task model AUROC > baseline AUROC on held-out test data) and generalization (performance consistent across time and patient subgroups), but does not establish causation (whether improved predictions would reduce readmissions if acted upon) — that would require a prospective randomized trial. The design does not control for unmeasured confounds (e.g., patient health literacy, social support) that may bias both model inputs and readmission outcomes.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Task Learning for 30-Day Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

We will use the complete 50,000-record dataset spanning 3 years of hospital discharges, including both structured EHR data (demographics, lab results, comorbidities, vital signs, medications, procedures) and unstructured clinical notes (discharge summaries, progress notes). The binary readmission label is defined as: patient readmitted to the same hospital system within 30 calendar days of index discharge (positive class) versus no readmission within 30 days (negative class). Readmissions are extracted from EHR admission records; planned readmissions (e.g., scheduled procedures) are explicitly excluded via procedure codes and clinical documentation flags. All labels are assigned at the time of discharge or inferred from subsequent admission records, ensuring ground truth is objectively observable.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

We employ a stratified temporal split to address both temporal validity and class imbalance. Discharges are ordered chronologically and divided into three windows: Training (first 18 months, ~30,000 records), Validation (months 19–24, ~10,000 records), Test (months 25–36, ~10,000 records). Within each temporal window, records are stratified by readmission status to preserve the readmission rate distribution across all three sets. This approach prevents information leakage: clinical outcomes and care practices evolve over time, and temporal ordering ensures future information does not contaminate training. Stratification ensures both classes are adequately represented despite imbalance (~15–20% readmission rate), preventing the training set from being dominated by negative examples. No patient record appears in multiple splits.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Structured features include ~40–60 engineered attributes: age, sex, admission type, primary and secondary diagnoses (encoded as ICD codes), comorbidity indices (Elixhauser or Charlson), laboratory values (most recent pre-discharge), vital signs (admission and discharge), medication and procedure counts, length of stay, and admission source. Unstructured clinical notes are processed as follows: (a) PHI removal and lowercasing; (b) tokenization and stopword removal; (c) representation as TF-IDF vectors or learned embeddings via a bidirectional LSTM or transformer encoder within the multi-task model.

All preprocessing transformers—text vectorizers, numeric scalers, categorical encoders, and imputation strategies—are fit exclusively on the training set, then applied deterministically to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents data leakage from validation/test distributions into the model. Features are validated to ensure no feature encodes the readmission label (e.g., post-discharge diagnosis codes are excluded) and all features reflect the clinical state at or before discharge.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The multi-task learning model uses a shared embedding layer that jointly encodes structured EHR data (via a dense subnetwork) and clinical notes (via an LSTM or transformer encoder). This shared representation feeds into task-specific heads: a primary head predicting 30-day readmission (binary classification) and auxiliary heads predicting 90-day readmission, length of stay, or comorbidity patterns. The auxiliary tasks encourage the learned representation to capture generalizable clinical patterns. Embedding dimension: 128–256; LSTM hidden units or transformer heads: 256–512. Regularization via dropout (0.3–0.5), L2 weight decay (1e−4 to 1e−3), and early stopping on validation loss.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on structured EHR data only—the "traditional separate modeling approach" mentioned in the hypothesis. The baseline receives equal tuning effort: hyperparameter search over regularization strength (C), class weight balancing to handle imbalance, and identical preprocessing on structured features. Both models are trained on the same training set and evaluated on the same validation/test sets. The baseline's restriction to structured features isolates the contribution of multi-task learning with joint embeddings.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). AUROC is threshold-agnostic, robust to class imbalance, and aligns with the clinical objective: accurate ranking of patients by readmission risk allows clinicians to target interventions on high-risk populations. Secondary metrics include Average Precision (captures performance on the positive class, important when false negatives carry high cost), calibration error (Brier score or expected calibration error, critical for clinical decision support), and sensitivity/specificity at clinically relevant operating points (e.g., sensitivity at 85% specificity). Additionally, we report sensitivity and specificity stratified by patient subgroups (age quartiles, admission type, comorbidity burden) to detect whether multi-task learning benefits apply uniformly or if certain populations are advantaged or disadvantaged.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping are performed on the validation set only. We perform grid or random search over model hyperparameters (embedding dimension, LSTM hidden units, dropout rate, L2 weight decay) and the baseline's regularization strength (C). The model checkpoint (or hyperparameter configuration) achieving the best validation AUROC is retained. The validation set is used iteratively during tuning but not for final performance reporting. The test set is held completely out-of-sample and touched only once at the end: final model evaluation occurs after all tuning is complete, and test set metrics are computed and reported without any additional iteration or threshold calibration.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

We address known confounds as follows: Hospital care practice variability is controlled via stratified analysis by hospital site (if multi-site data) or time period, reporting per-site or per-period AUROC to verify that the multi-task learning benefit is consistent. Socio-economic factors are represented through proxy variables (insurance type, admission source, neighborhood deprivation indices if available) included in both multi-task and baseline models, ensuring fair comparison. Baseline characteristics (age, sex, comorbidity burden, readmission rate) are reported stratified by train/val/test sets to verify balance and detect case-mix shift.

This design establishes association and generalization—whether multi-task learning with joint embeddings achieves higher AUROC than structured-only modeling on held-out test data—but does not establish causation (i.e., whether improved predictions would reduce readmissions if acted upon, which would require a prospective trial). Unmeasured confounds (e.g., patient health literacy, social support) that influence both model inputs and readmission outcomes are not controlled for and may bias results.
